evidence: Extra lowercase 'the' inserted between period and opening quote mark in DB version. PDF source shows 'is conclusive.' followed by a line break and then the opening quote for 'Pursuant'. The 'the' appears to be an OCR or ingestion artifact introduced during conversion.
note: Located in ¶23, within the long block quote of the district court's opinion. The extra 'the' breaks the sentence structure—should go directly from 'is conclusive.' to the quoted text '"Pursuant'.

old_exact: applies to the Industrial Commission's Wilkinsons' property, and determination
new_exact: applies to the Wilkinsons' property, and the Industrial Commission's determination
source_quote:
As a matter of law, Chpt. 61-33.1, N.D.C.C., applies to the
      Wilkinsons' property, and the Industrial Commission's
      determination that the Wilkinsons' minerals are above the OHWM
      of the historical Missouri riverbed channel is conclusive.
evidence: DB version presents a grammatically malformed phrase: 'applies to the Industrial Commission's Wilkinsons' property, and determination' creates a nonsensical subject-object relationship. PDF source clearly shows the phrase correctly spread across multiple lines: 'applies to the Wilkinsons' property, and the Industrial Commission's determination'. The DB version joined these lines incorrectly, transposing the phrases and creating a failure of agreement.
note: Located in ¶23, within the long block quote of the district court's opinion. The transposition is a line-joining artifact: the correct reading is 'the chapter applies to the Wilkinsons' property, and the Industrial Commission's [court] determination [is] that the minerals are above OHWM'. The DB version inverts this, placing 'Industrial Commission's' before 'Wilkinsons'' and 'determination' after the copula incorrectly.

evidence: Math-mode markup ($...$) and LaTeX backslash escapes (\) are ingestion artifacts from markdown preprocessing. The authoritative PDF source shows clean plain-text citation format without markup contamination. This is a known class of contamination handled by a separate deterministic pass per rule 11.
note: Markup contamination: isolated math-mode delimiters and LaTeX backslashes (\v. and \d.a.) in case citation. All words present in source.

evidence: The DB contains a spaced-out ellipsis (. . . . . ) with 5 dots and spaces between them in paragraph 10, where the authoritative PDF source shows a compact 4-dot ellipsis (....) on its own line. This is an ingestion/OCR artifact where the PDF's compact ellipsis was incorrectly transcribed as spaced dots. The context is in the district court's quoted statement within paragraph 10 of the opinion.
note: Paragraph 10 contains a block quote from the district court's order. The ellipsis marks an omission within that quoted material. The PDF rendering shows the standard ND Supreme Court format (compact 4-dot ellipsis) while the DB transcription expanded it into spaced dots. This proposal converts it to the standard compact form that matches the printed opinion.

source_quote: N.D.C.C. ch. 28-20.1 because Carol Beck was not notified of the filing of the Maryland custody decree as required under § 28-20.1-03(2) and because a ten-day period had not elapsed before the court issued its ex parte order as required under § 28-20.1-03(3).
note: The DB text fused 'ten' and 'day' into 'tenday', removing the hyphen. The authoritative PDF source renders this as 'ten-day' (hyphenated), with the line break splitting it as 'ten-' on line 227 and 'day period' on line 228 of the pdftotext output. This is an OCR/ingestion defect where the hyphen and word separation were not preserved.

source: pdftotext -layout extraction from /Users/jerod/refs/nd/opin/pdfs/2020/2020ND93.pdf; visual verification via mutool draw page 6 paragraph [¶11]
note: The PDF source text shows the statute range split across lines as '28-\n32-42-49' (page 5 line end shows '28-' and page 6 line start shows '32-42-49'). When joined, this forms the correct compact range notation '28-32-42-49'. The DB incorrectly introduces a space before the final dash, producing '28-32-42 -49', which is an OCR/ingestion artifact. The correct form is confirmed by visual rendering of the PDF and is standard legal citation format for statute ranges.

evidence: The database contains a spaced ellipsis (. . . .) at line 168, which represents omitted statutory text in N.D.C.C. § 27-10-01.1. The authoritative PDF source (when rendered with pdftotext -layout) shows this same omitted text as '.     .         .   .' (with variable spacing). According to ND court publishing standards, ellipses should use the compact four-dot form '....'. The current DB form is an ingestion/OCR spacing artifact that should be compacted.
note: This is the opinion quoting omitted portions of statute N.D.C.C. § 27-10-01.1 (definitions 1 and 2). The compact form matches the standard ND Supreme Court ellipsis formatting.
